From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Clemson-class destroyer

For other ships with the same name, see [USS Rodgers](/wiki/USS_Rodgers "USS Rodgers").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
|  | |
|  | |
|  | |
|  | |
| History | |
|  | |

| Name | USS *Rodgers* |
| Namesake | [John Rodgers (1812–1882)](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(American_Civil_War_naval_officer) "John Rodgers (American Civil War naval officer)") |
| Builder | [Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation "Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation"), [Fore River Shipyard](/wiki/Fore_River_Shipyard "Fore River Shipyard"), [Quincy](/wiki/Quincy,_Massachusetts "Quincy, Massachusetts") |
| Laid down | 25 September 1918 |

| General characteristics | |
| Class & type | [*Clemson*-class](/wiki/Clemson-class_destroyer "Clemson-class destroyer") [destroyer](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer") |
| Displacement | 1,190 tons |
| Length | 314 ft 5 in (95.83 m) |
| Beam | 31 ft (9.4 m) |
| Draft | 9 ft 10 in (3.00 m) |
| Propulsion | * 26,500 shp (19,800 kW) * geared turbines, * 2 screws |
| Speed | 34.4 kn (63.7 km/h; 39.6 mph) |
| Range | 4,900 nmi (9,100 km; 5,600 mi) at 15 kn (28 km/h; 17 mph) |
| Complement | 131 officers and enlisted |
| Armament | * 4 x [4 in (100 mm)](/wiki/4%22/50_caliber_gun "4\"/50 caliber gun") guns * 1 x [3 in (76 mm)](/wiki/3%22/23_caliber_gun "3\"/23 caliber gun") gun * 12 x [21 inch (533 mm)](/wiki/American_21_inch_torpedo "American 21 inch torpedo") [torpedo tubes](/wiki/Torpedo_tube "Torpedo tube") |
|  | |

The third **USS *Rodgers* (DD-254)** was a [*Clemson*-class](/wiki/Clemson-class_destroyer "Clemson-class destroyer") [destroyer](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer") in the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"), transferred to the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") and served as **HMS *Sherwood* (I80)** during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

## Service history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=USS_Rodgers_(DD-254)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Service history")]

### As USS *Rodgers*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=USS_Rodgers_(DD-254)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: As USS Rodgers")]

She was named for [John Rodgers (1772–1838)](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_War_of_1812) "John Rodgers (naval officer, War of 1812)"), his son, [John Rodgers (1812–1882)](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_Civil_War) "John Rodgers (naval officer, Civil War)") and his great grandson, [John Rodgers (1881–1926)](/wiki/John_Rodgers_(naval_officer,_World_War_I) "John Rodgers (naval officer, World War I)").

*Rodgers* was [laid down](/wiki/Keel_laying "Keel laying") as *Kalk* 25 September 1918 by the [Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/Bethlehem_Shipbuilding_Corporation "Bethlehem Shipbuilding Corporation"); renamed *Rodgers* 23 December 1918; [launched](/wiki/Ship_naming_and_launching "Ship naming and launching") 26 April 1919; sponsored by Miss Helen T. Rodgers, granddaughter of Commodore John Rodgers; and [commissioned](/wiki/Ship_commissioning "Ship commissioning") 22 July 1919.

*Rodgers* served with Division 28, Destroyers, [Atlantic Fleet](/wiki/U.S._Atlantic_Fleet "U.S. Atlantic Fleet"), until the spring of 1922 when she steamed to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") for inactivation. [Decommissioned](/wiki/Ship_decommissioning "Ship decommissioning") 20 July of that year, she remained in reserve until after the outbreak of World War II in Europe.

*Rodgers*, recommissioned 18 December 1939, again served briefly with the Atlantic Fleet, and in October 1940 moved to [Halifax, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia") where she joined other destroyers being transferred to the United Kingdom in [exchange for bases](/wiki/Destroyers_for_Bases_Agreement "Destroyers for Bases Agreement") in the Western Hemisphere.

### As HMS *Sherwood*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=USS_Rodgers_(DD-254)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: As HMS Sherwood")]

She decommissioned 23 October 1940 and was transferred and commissioned the same day for service in the 4th "Town" Flotilla as HMS *Sherwood* (I80).

*Sherwood* sailed for the United Kingdom 1 November. Diverted en route, she participated in the search for survivors of ships lost from convoy HX 84 and in the subsequent hunt for the [German cruiser *Admiral Scheer*](/wiki/German_cruiser_Admiral_Scheer "German cruiser Admiral Scheer"), when returning to Canada for repairs. On 18 November, she arrived at [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), continued on to [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth"), from where, after overhaul, she sailed to join the 12th Escort Group, [Western Approaches Command](/wiki/Western_Approaches_Command "Western Approaches Command") at [Londonderry Port](/wiki/Londonderry_Port "Londonderry Port"). Transferred, with her group, to [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") in April 1941, she joined in the hunt for the [German battleship *Bismarck*](/wiki/German_battleship_Bismarck "German battleship Bismarck") in May. On 28 May, the day after the German battleship had been sunk, the ship assisted in rescue operations for survivors from British destroyer [HMS *Mashona*](/wiki/HMS_Mashona "HMS Mashona").

During the summer, *Sherwood* underwent repairs in the [Clyde](/wiki/River_Clyde "River Clyde"), and then returned to Londonderry Port, from where she operated, first with the 2nd Escort Group, then with the 22nd, into the new year, 1942. In February and March, she accompanied carriers during trials, and, after another yard period, April to August, served as a target ship for training aircraft from the Royal Naval Air Station at [Fearn](/wiki/RNAS_Fearn_(HMS_Owl) "RNAS Fearn (HMS Owl)"), Scotland. In the autumn, she again crossed the Atlantic and served with the Newfoundland Command until she returned to Londonderry Port in February 1943. During March and April she escorted a convoy to [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia") and back, but by May she again needed major repairs. Worn out, she was taken out of active service at [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham_Dockyard "Chatham Dockyard"), stripped of usable parts and ordnance and towed to the Humber where she was beached in shallow water for use as an aircraft target. Her hulk was scrapped in 1945.
